text:
CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH AND WITHOUT INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 63-year-old male with metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma with sarcomatoid differentiation, status post right radical cytoreductive nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection. Currently on nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks (maintenance, following 4-cycle induction with ipilimumab/nivolumab). Prior imaging demonstrated discordant/mixed response with pulmonary nodules responding but retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal disease enlarging. This study is obtained for disease restaging and treatment response assessment.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed using a standard oncologic protocol. Images were acquired in the axial plane without and with IV contrast (portal venous phase through abdomen and pelvis; 80 mL Omnipaque 350 administered via peripheral IV at 3 mL/sec). Multiplanar reformations in coronal and sagittal planes were generated and reviewed. Oral contrast was administered. No known contrast allergy. Comparison made to prior CT from approximately 6 weeks prior and earlier studies.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The airways are patent to the subsegmental level bilaterally. No endobronchial lesions identified. The lungs are well-inflated bilaterally.

Pulmonary Nodules: The previously tracked pulmonary nodules demonstrate overall stability compared to the most recent prior examination:
- Right lower lobe: A well-circumscribed, solid pulmonary nodule is again identified in the posterior basal segment of the right lower lobe, measuring 0.8 cm in greatest axial dimension (previously 0.7 cm on the 6-week prior scan, 1.4 cm at baseline prior to initiation of systemic therapy). This represents a minimal interval increase over the short-interval follow-up period; however, the nodule remains markedly smaller than its baseline size, indicating continued durable treatment effect from prior immunotherapy induction. The nodule maintains smooth margins and there is no associated pleural tethering or satellite nodularity.
- Left lower lobe: A subpleural nodule in the posterior basal segment of the left lower lobe measures 0.8 cm (previously 0.7 cm). Minimal change, consistent with overall stability within measurement variability. No new satellite nodules in this lobe.
- Right upper lobe: A small nodule in the posterior segment of the right upper lobe measures 0.3 cm (unchanged from prior). This nodule is at the lower limit of reliable CT measurement; no change.
- No new pulmonary nodules are identified to suggest emergence of new sites of pulmonary metastatic disease.
- No cavitation, ground-glass opacity halo, or other features to suggest superimposed infection or pneumonitis.

Pleura: No pleural effusions bilaterally. No pneumothorax. No pleural thickening or masses to suggest pleural metastatic involvement.

Mediastinum and Hilum: The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber without dissection or aneurysm. The superior vena cava and pulmonary vasculature are unremarkable. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy above the standard size threshold. A few small prevascular and paratracheal nodes are present, the largest approximately 0.8 cm short axis, which are unchanged and nonspecific.

Chest Wall and Thoracic Skeleton: No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions in the ribs, sternum, or thoracic vertebrae. No pathologic fractures identified.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size and contour. No focal hepatic lesions identified on pre- or post-contrast imaging. Hepatic parenchyma enhances homogeneously without areas of hypo- or hyperenhancement to suggest hepatic metastases. Hepatic veins and portal vein are patent. No biliary ductal dilatation. No pericholecystic fluid. The gallbladder appears normal.

Pancreas: The pancreas is homogeneous in attenuation without focal masses or ductal dilatation. Normal enhancement pattern.

Spleen: The spleen is within normal limits in size (craniocaudal span approximately 11 cm). No splenic lesions. Splenomegaly not identified.

Kidneys and Adrenal Glands: The right nephrectomy bed demonstrates expected post-surgical changes, with soft tissue stranding and fibrosis in the right renal fossa. No discrete nodular soft tissue mass or locally recurrent disease is identified in the nephrectomy bed. The right adrenal gland was resected at the time of surgery; the expected absence of right adrenal tissue is noted. The left kidney is normal in size and morphology, with symmetric nephrographic enhancement and no hydronephrosis. No left-sided perinephric mass. Left adrenal gland is unremarkable.

Retroperitoneum: This is the area of primary concern on this examination.
- Aortocaval region: A nodal conglomerate mass in the aortocaval region, previously noted to measure 2.4 cm on the prior scan, now measures approximately 3.4 cm in greatest axial dimension (short axis approximately 2.8 cm). This represents definite interval growth over approximately 6 weeks. The mass demonstrates mild heterogeneous enhancement. There is no compression of the IVC or aorta at this level, though the nodal mass is in close proximity to the anterior wall of the IVC. The IVC remains patent with normal flow characteristics.
- Para-aortic / right common iliac region: A dominant nodal mass in the right common iliac chain previously measured 2.0 cm and now measures 3.1 cm in greatest axial dimension (short axis approximately 2.4 cm). This nodal mass has increased substantially since the prior scan and now shows mild internal heterogeneity. No central necrosis is appreciated on this examination.

Gastrointestinal Tract: The stomach, small bowel, and colon are unremarkable. No bowel obstruction or intraluminal mass. No mesenteric or omental nodularity to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. Appendix is not visualized but no periappendiceal inflammatory changes are present.

Ascites: No free intraperitoneal fluid.

---

PELVIS:

Pelvic Lymph Nodes: The pelvic nodal disease has continued to progress since the preceding study:
- Right obturator node: Previously measured 1.1 cm, now measures 1.8 cm. The node is rounded, and its short axis has increased substantially, consistent with ongoing metastatic nodal infiltration. This node abuts but does not compress the adjacent obturator neurovascular bundle.
- Right external iliac node: A new discrete rounded lymph node measuring 1.2 cm in short axis is identified along the right external iliac chain. This node was not present on the prior study and represents new metastatic nodal involvement. No necrosis.
- Left pelvic sidewall: No convincingly enlarged lymph nodes on the left side. A 0.6 cm node is noted in the left external iliac chain, which is below the standard size threshold and of uncertain significance; no change from prior.

Bladder: The urinary bladder is adequately distended and demonstrates a smooth, uniform wall without mass or intraluminal filling defect. No hydronephrosis to suggest distal ureteral obstruction from pelvic nodal disease.

Prostate and Seminal Vesicles: The prostate gland is mildly enlarged but without focal lesion. Seminal vesicles are symmetric and unremarkable.

Pelvic Musculature and Skeleton: The pelvic musculature is intact and symmetric. The visualized iliac, sacral, and proximal femoral osseous structures demonstrate normal trabecular pattern without aggressive periosteal reaction or frank lytic/sclerotic lesion to suggest osseous metastatic disease. No pathologic fractures. (Note: a 0.5 cm sclerotic focus was not yet identified at this examination stage; this study predates the bone scan evaluation.)

Pelvic Vasculature: The iliac vessels are patent and of normal caliber bilaterally. The inferior vena cava and infrarenal aorta are unremarkable.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. RETROPERITONEAL AND PELVIC NODAL DISEASE -- INTERVAL PROGRESSION. The retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal disease has continued to progress compared to the most recent prior examination obtained approximately 6 weeks ago, and represents unequivocal enlargement from the nadir measurements obtained after immunotherapy induction. The aortocaval nodal conglomerate has grown from 2.4 cm to 3.4 cm, the right common iliac nodal mass from 2.0 cm to 3.1 cm, and the right obturator node from 1.1 cm to 1.8 cm. A new right external iliac chain node measuring 1.2 cm is now apparent, indicating extension of nodal metastatic disease further into the right hemipelvis. Taken together, the overall nodal burden has grown substantially.

2. PULMONARY NODULES -- STABLE / MINIMAL CHANGE. The bilateral pulmonary nodules tracked since baseline remain dramatically smaller than at initial diagnosis, reflecting the partial response observed during immunotherapy induction. On this study, the nodules are essentially stable when accounting for measurement variability (right lower lobe 0.8 cm, left lower lobe 0.8 cm, right upper lobe 0.3 cm). There is no new pulmonary metastatic disease.

3. NO HEPATIC METASTASES. The liver enhances homogeneously without focal lesion.

4. NO OSSEOUS METASTASES IDENTIFIED ON THIS STUDY. No aggressive osseous lesions are seen on this non-dedicated bone assessment; pelvic and thoracic skeletal structures appear intact.

5. POST-NEPHRECTOMY CHANGES. Expected postsurgical changes in the right renal fossa without convincing evidence of local tumor recurrence at this time.

6. OVERALL DISEASE TRAJECTORY: The ongoing discordant pattern of immunotherapy response -- in which pulmonary metastases have responded and stabilized while retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal disease has progressed through first-line nivolumab maintenance -- is confirmed on this study. The cumulative nodal progression, particularly with appearance of a new right external iliac node, suggests inadequate disease control in the lymphatic compartment on current therapy.
